The scrollbar bug I mentioned in my practice test review wasn't present during this assessment. The allotted time for this assessment seemed a bit low but maybe that's just me. I think 30 minutes per task is a more reasonable estimate. The front-end debugging problem seemed a bit unrealistic. In a normal debugging situation you would at least have the undesired behavior listed. The desired behavior was listed but undesired behavior (the bugs) weren't and had to be discovered by the user. Also if a user is required to log in they should be automatically logged in so that time is solely spent on debugging the issue and not set-up steps. It might just be hardware limitations of my computer but accessing the dev console for the front end portion of the assessment was fairly laggy.

You mention that looking up syntax is ok, yet require full screen mode and a single monitor so It's not really clear how syntax related look-ups should be performed efficiently. Looking up syntax on a phone is really inefficient and slow compared to the equivalent on a computer.

Also, when typing my feedback in this text box it keeps randomly losing focus without any additional input from me. Unfortunately because of the random nature I can't provide a good way to reproduce the issue.

I'm really glad the scrollbar issue from the practice test wasn't present during this assessment. I also like the addition of the AI agent although it doesn't provide the best answers. If we're going to be using fully capable AI in our work why shouldn't we be able to use fully capable AI during our assessment? I think the AI assistant should be less handicapped.

n my opinion, the test is adequate for evaluating a Java and Spring Boot developer. Part 1, which focuses on core Java, is a good exercise. The allotted time is sufficient to complete the solution, and the problem can be approached either through core Java implementation or by using Streams, which helps assess the candidate’s depth of expertise.

For Part 2, the Spring Boot section, I was unfortunately unable to fully focus due to personal matters. That aside, I think it is a well-designed exercise that effectively evaluates a candidate’s understanding of the required architecture and their ability to correctly implement and configure the necessary beans. The time provided should also be enough for candidates to deliver a working solution.

One comment regarding the IDE: while the AI assistance can be helpful, it can also be confusing at times. I also found it somewhat challenging to navigate the project structure. I’m not sure if there was a walkthrough of the workspace features, but if there wasn’t, providing a brief guide or orientation would be beneficial for future candidates.

Couple of things:
1. The intellisense on the last coding problem stopped working and it cost me around 10 minutes to get it working again.
2. The inability to debug properly by placing break points and inspecting values is a problem, since putting console.writeline or printf statements into the code is time consuming, and does not reflect the true developer experience.
3. When my intellisense failed, I tried to use ALT+SPACE to try and trigger it, which is normal for any editor. However, myorganisation has the Copilot app installed on my windows machine, and its bound to the ALT+SPACE hot key, which in turn had the copilot chat window pop up. This can cause false positives with regards to irregularities.
4. On the final coding question I got, it was not clear if I should be randomly reorganising the array according to all possible combinations, or whether it would be passed in. The examples never showed reorganisation as part of the algorithm to be performed, except on the first step. It says 'the mamimum is 7', but fails to show an example of which combination actually makes up 7, so this is incredibly confusing. I think that question needs to be relooked at to ensure the participant understands what is excepted more clearly.
